coredata.dc:title: A pH-controlled synthetic route to violet, green, and orange fluorescent carbon dots for multicolor light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Ruoyu
Chen, Xipao
Ouyang, Ning
Hu, Yaoping
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with tunable photoluminescence (PL), especially with long wavelength PL, offer great potential for various applications including sensing, bioimaging, photomedicine, and optoelectronic devices. Here, we use 2,3-diaminopyridine as a single precursor to synthesize colorful CDs in different pH conditions. By simply regulating the reaction media from alkali to neutral to acid, bright CDs emitting violet, green, and orange fluorescence are prepared, respectively. Detailed characterizations reveal that the red-shift of PL is caused by the growing size of sp2 conjugated domain and increasing content of graphite nitrogen. We embed CDs in starch to suppressself-quenchingand enhance the PL quantum yields. The CD/starch composites, possessing efficient solid-state fluorescence, can be coated on the ultraviolet chips (365 nm) to fabricate multicolor light-emitting diodes toward indoor illumination. This work presents a new strategy to adjust the PL wavelength of CDs in a wide range, and clarifies the emission mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: pH-controlled route
Carbon dots
Tunable photoluminescence
Emission mechanism
Multicolor light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphologies, structures, and components of CDs
Possible emission mechanism and formation mechanism of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption, optimal PL, and optimal PL excitation spectra of v-CDs (a), g-CDs (b), and o-CDs (c). Insets in (a–c): photographs of aqueous CD solution in daylight and under the optimal excitation wavelengths. Under different excitation wavelengths, the PL spectra of v-CDs (d), g-CDs (e), and o-CDs (f).

TEM images of v-CDs (a), g-CDs (b), and o-CDs (c). Insets in (a–c): size distributions and high-resolution TEM images of three CDs. (d) Raman, (e) 13C NMR, (f) 1H NMR, (g) TGA, (h) FTIR, and (i) XPS scan spectra of three CDs.
XPS High-resolution C1s (a, b, c) and N1s (d, e, f) spectra of v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs.
Schematic illustration of the emission wavelength controlled by the size of sp2 conjugated domain and the amount of graphite nitrogen.

PL spectra of the v-CD/starch composite (a), g-CD/starch composite (b), and o-CD/starch composite (c). Insets in (a–c): photographs of the CD/starch composites in daylight and under irradiation of a 365-nm UV lamp. PL spectra of multicolor LEDs based on v-CD/starch (d), g-CD/starch (e), and o-CD/starch (f). (g–i) The corresponding CIE color coordinates of the CD/starch based LEDs.
Data analysis of XPS C1s and N1s spectra of v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs.

head.ce:title: A pH-controlled synthetic route to violet, green, and orange fluorescent carbon dots for multicolor light-emitting diodes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with tunable photoluminescence (PL), especially with long wavelength PL, offer great potential for various applications including sensing, bioimaging, photomedicine, and optoelectronic devices. Here, we use 2,3-diaminopyridine as a single precursor to synthesize colorful CDs in different pH conditions. By simply regulating the reaction media from alkali to neutral to acid, bright CDs emitting violet, green, and orange fluorescence are prepared, respectively. Detailed characterizations reveal that the red-shift of PL is caused by the growing size of sp2 conjugated domain and increasing content of graphite nitrogen. We embed CDs in starch to suppressself-quenchingand enhance the PL quantum yields. The CD/starch composites, possessing efficient solid-state fluorescence, can be coated on the ultraviolet chips (365 nm) to fabricate multicolor light-emitting diodes toward indoor illumination. This work presents a new strategy to adjust the PL wavelength of CDs in a wide range, and clarifies the emission mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) with nanoscopic size smaller than 10 nm have gained huge interest because of their extraordinary optical and physicochemical properties that are attractive for applications in sensing, bioimaging, photomedicine, catalysis, information encryption, and optoelectronic devices [1–6]. One of the most appealing characters of CDs is their tunable photoluminescence (PL). Unfortunately, a large majority of CDs only emit strong blue or green light in the short-wavelength region (420–530 nm) [7,8]. These CDs typically possess an excitation dependent feature, exhibiting very weak PL intensity in the band of 550–700 nm. Long-wavelength emissive CDs with intensive orange or red fluorescence are less reported due to the lack of efficient synthetic strategy and ambiguity of PL mechanism, which severely limits the utilization of CDs in various fields, especially in deep-tissue imaging and full-color light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [9–11].

Recently, substantial efforts have been made to adjust the PL of CDs across the entire visible spectrum and elucidate the origin of emission [12–20]. Pang group developed a wet oxidation method to prepared CDs from carbon fibers. After ultrafiltration, the separated CD fractions with molecular weights of < 3, 3–10, and 10–30 kDa showed emission peaks at 526, 570, and 608 nm, respectively. The authors demonstrated that the fluorescence of CDs was mainly affected by the particle size and surface oxidation state [12]. Jiang et al. fabricated blue, green, and red CDs from three isomers of phenylenediamines, and revealed that the alteration in PL originated from the difference in particle size and nitrogen content [13]. Xiong group obtained CDs emitting luminescence in gradient colors from blue to red via hydrothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine and urea followed by silica column chromatography purification. They found that the band gap of CDs was tuned by the surface groups and structures, not depended on the particle size [14]. These pioneering works are truly impressive. However, the development of multicolor emitting CDs is still at early stage, and the corresponding PL mechanisms are debatable and even contradictory due to various synthetic methods used and complex parameters considered together. Therefore, it remains a big challenge to design controlled synthetic strategies toward CDs with tunable PL (particularly emitting long-wavelength fluorescence), as well as clarify the relationships between their structures and PL properties.

Herein, we introduced a pH-controlled route to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs with strong violet, green, and orange fluorescence via hydrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminopyridine, a single precursor, in NaOH, pure water, and HCl media, respectively. Systematic investigations indicate that the larger size of conjugated sp2 domain and the increased amount of graphitic nitrogen narrow down the band gap of CDs, leading to the red-shift of PL to the low energy end in the visible spectrum. In addition, embedding CDs in starch is carried out to enhance the emission quantum yields, and realize efficient solid-state fluorescence. These CD/starch composites hold great promise in multicolor LEDs.

2,3-Diaminopyridine and starch were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. All other chemicals including sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Deionized water got from a Millipore purification system was used throughout the experiments.

Multicolor CDs were prepared by a pH-controlled hydrothermal method. For a typical synthesis of orange emissive CDs (o-CDs), 50 mg of 2,3-diaminopyridine was dispersed in 30 mL of 1 mol L-1 HCl solution (pH = 0 before addition of the precursor). After 10-minute sonification, the mixture was transferred to a 50-mL autoclave and heated for 6 h under 180 °C to produce o-CDs. For purification, the obtained o-CD suspension was successively centrifuged (8000 rpm, 10 min), filtered (0.22-μm membrane), and dialyzed (molecular weight cut off 1000 Da, 2 days). Finally, the product was freeze-dried to afford the o-CD powder. Hydrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminopyridine (50 mg) in pure water (30 mL, pH = 7 before addition of the precursor) and NaOH solution (30 mL, 1 mol L−1, pH = 14 before addition of the precursor) produced green and violet emissive CDs (g-CDs and v-CDs), respectively. The production yields of CDs are 5–8% by direct weight transfer of the precursor to the nanomaterial.

In a typical run, 100 mg of o-CDs and 400 mg of starch were added into a 100-mL beaker containing 50 mL of water. After constant stirring for 8 h at room temperature, the mixture was filtered to remove the unadsorbed o-CDs in water. The remaining solid o-CD/starch composite was collected and vacuum-dried at 60 °C overnight for further measurements. Using v-CDs and g-CDs instead of o-CDs, other two CD/starch composites were prepared.

Briefly, the commercial InGaN chip (365 nm) was fully coated with the CD/starch composite. The AB glue were employed as the cementing agent. After drying in air naturally, the CD/starch based LED was fabricated. The working voltage and current of the LED lamp were set at 4.4 V and 250 mA, respectively.

The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were monitored by UV-3101PC and Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometers. The absolute quantum yields (QYs) were tested using the QE-2100 quantum efficiency measurement system. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were taken on a Horiba FL3-111 spectrometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CDs were observed on a JEM-2100 apparatus operating at 200 kV. The Raman spectra were measured using a 532 nm laser on a Renishaw inVia-reflex spectroscopy. The 13C NMR and 1H NMR spectra were carried out with a Bruker Avance III 600 spectrometer using DMSO‑d 6 as the solvent. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a NETZSCH TGA5500 instrument under nitrogen atmosphere. The temperature range and heating rate were 30–750 °C and 10 °C min−1, respectively. The Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were made on an AXIS UL TRA DLD system. The PL spectra of the LEDs were measured by a Spectrascan Model PR-650 spectrophotometer with an integral sphere.

Three batches of CDs prepared in NaOH, pure water, and HCl aqueous solutions are named as v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs, which exhibit brown, yellow, and pink colors in daylight, respectively (insets in Fig. 1 a–c, left side). The UV–Vis absorption spectra show that, in the UV region, each sample has a shoulder at about 240 nm and a well-resolved peak at around 300 nm (Fig. 1a–c), which correspond to the π–π* transitions of C = C/C = N in the aromatic carbon clusters and the n–π* transitions of CO/CN bonds, respectively [21,22]. In a close-up view, the shoulder moves from 231 to 240 to 246 nm and the peak shifts from 293 to 307 to 315 nm accompanied by the enhanced absorption for v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs, indicating that the size of sp2 domain in the core increases [23]. In the visible region, different from v-CDs, g-CDs and o-CDs have additional weak broad bands at 400 and 538 nm, respectively, which are related with the surface conjugated groups attached to the edges of inner cores that further decrease the band gaps of the fluorescent materials [24]. It is found that the PL excitation spectra of CDs are strongly associated with the lower energy absorption bands, as their wavelength regions are well overlapped (the PL excitation peaks are located at 310 nm for v-CDs, 416 nm for g-CDs, and 537 nm for o-CDs). Under the optimal excitation wavelengths, v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs emit bright violet, green, and orange fluorescence (insets in Fig. 1a–c, right side) with PL peaks centered at 382, 516, and 594 nm, respectively. The emission behaviors of all three CDs depend on excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1d–f), which may reflect the fact that not only different sp2 domain sizes but also diverse nitrogen-related defect states exist in each CD sample [25,26]. The absolute PL quantum yields of v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs in aqueous solution are determined to be 8.4%, 9.3%, and 8.3%, respectively, by using an integrating sphere. The time-resolved PL measurements are carried out to further investigate the optical properties of CDs. As illustrated in Fig. S1, all three decay profiles fit monoexponential functions, givinglifetimes of 4.60, 4.53, and 2.98 ns for v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs, respectively. Such short and descending lifetimes imply that the recombination pathways are dominated by the core states [27], and larger sp2 domain induces faster fluorescence decay. The PL intensities of all three CDs keep almost constant after three-month storage at room temperature (Fig. S2), indicating the good optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies, structures, and components of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a–c show the representative TEM images of as-prepared samples. It can be seen that all the materials are dot-shaped and finely dispersed without obvious aggregation. Their sizes are uniform, conforming to the Gaussian distribution (insets in Fig. 2a–c, lower left). The average diameters of v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs are estimated to be 2.6, 3.5, and 4.5 nm, respectively, by counting more than 200 particles. This result proves that the increasing size leads to the red-shift of PL wavelength. The high-resolution TEM images (insets in Fig. 2a–c, upper right) reveal the amorphous nature of all three CDs due to the absence of discernible lattice fringes. We speculate that the inside aromatic sp2 carbon clusters oriented in a considerably random fashion make CDs noncrystalline [28]. Fig. 2d records the Raman spectra of three CDs. D bands at 1370 cm−1 and G bands at 1550 cm−1 are presented, which are ascribed to the disordered sp3 carbons and the graphite sp2 coordinated rings, respectively [29]. The relative intensity of D to G band (ID/IG) increases from 1.06 to 1.18 to 1.31 for v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs, demonstrating that CDs emitting longer wavelength PL contain a larger amount of structural defects caused by the increased number of graphitic nitrogen atoms embedded in sp2 networks [30]. NMR technique is used to analyze the hybridization states of carbon. In the 13C NMR spectra (Fig. 2e), the signals at 10–80 and 100–160 ppm are assigned to the sp3-hybridized C–C/C–N/C–O carbons and the sp2-hybridized CC/CN aromatic carbons, respectively [31]. The 1H NMR spectra (Fig. 2f) depict the sp3 C–H protons in 1–3 ppm, the protons attached to hydroxyl, amino, ether and carbonyl groups in 3–5 ppm, and the aromatic protons in 6–8 ppm [32]. For comparison, both 13C and 1H NMR data show an increase in relative intensity of conjugated sp2 domains to non-conjugated sp3 structures in the order of v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs. Thermostability of the nanomaterials are studied by TGA under N2 atmosphere. As illustrated in Fig. 2g, v-CDs possess four decomposition stages. The initial lost component before 160 °C arisesfrom the evaporation of H2O adsorbed on CDs or formed in the dehydration process [33]. The mass loss during the second stage in 160–350 °C corresponds to the decomposition of functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, and amino on CD surface. The third stage from 350 to 550 °C is attributed to the pyrolysis of sp3 carbons in the core, and the fourth stage in 550–750 °C may be resulted from the degradation of oxidized sp2 structures [34]. Different from v-CDs, both g-CDs and o-CDs exhibit three decomposition stages. The first stage before 120 °C with a slight mass loss of about 3% is caused by the evaporation of H2O adsorbed on CDs. The second stage in 120–410 °C reveals a massive mass loss of 60%–70% due to the decomposition of abundant surface groups. The third stage in the temperature range of 410–750 °C is ascribed to the decomposition of sp3 carbon species or other organic-like structures in the core of CDs. The residues are highly thermostable polyaromatic substances. It is observed that the remained content of the residues increases from 10.9% to 20.8% to 28.1% for v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs, respectively, meaning that o-CDs possess the greatest deal of rigid sp2 conjugated networks.

The chemical constituents and elemental states of CDs are further studied by FTIR and XPS. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2h), all three CDs present the characteristic stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H in the region of 3100–3450 cm−1, C–H at around 2825 cm−1, C–N at 1401 cm−1, and C–O ranging from 1080 to 1160 cm−1 [35,36]. It is found that g-CDs and o-CDs possess the narrow absorption band of CO at 1661 cm−1; but for v-CDs, the absorption band moves to 1640 cm−1 and becomes broader, which indicates the presence of carboxylate group (COONa) in the surface of v-CDs. In addition, compared with v-CDs and g-CDs, the aromatic CC at 1573 cm−1 and CN at 1622 cm−1 in o-CDs are more pronounced, suggesting that o-CDs contain the improved content of polyaromatic structures. The XPS full scan spectra in Fig. 2i show that all the CDs are mainly composed of C, N, and O. The atomic concentrations of different elements are listed in Table S1. A tiny amount of Cl and the element of Na are also detected in o-CDs and v-CDs, respectively, which are derived from the starting materials of HCl and NaOH. The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 3 a–c) exhibit four carbon species, including C–C/CC at 284.4 eV, C–N at 285.7 eV, C–O at 286.5 eV, and CO/CN at 288.5 eV [37]. Moreover, the proportion of C–C/CC in the C1s spectrum increases from 40.8% to 52.6% to 61.5% for v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs, respectively (Table 1 ), which confirms that the size of sp2 domain increases along with the red shift of PL wavelength. This result is in good agreement with the NMR analysis. The N1s spectra (Fig. 3d–f) can bedeconvoluted into four peaks at 398.5, 399.3, 400.3, and 401.3 eV, ascribed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and NH2, respectively [38,39]. The content of graphitic N is another important parameter responsible for the red shift of PL [30], which increases from 9.3% in v-CDs to 17.8% in g-CDs to 22.9% in o-CDs (Table 1). The O1s spectra (Fig. S3) describe two oxygen types of CO at 532.1 eV and C–O at 532.9 eV in all three CDs [40]. Additionally, v-CDs displays a signal of COONa at 536.0 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible emission mechanism and formation mechanism of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We calculate the band gap of CDs based on the equation Eg opt = 1240/λedge, where λedge corresponds to the onset value of the first excitonic absorption peak in the direction of longer wavelength [17]. In the order of v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs, the calculated band gap gradually decreases from 3.56 to 2.50 to 2.06 eV with the emission peak red-shifting from 382 to 516 to 594 nm and the size increasing from 2.6 to 3.5 to 4.5 nm, which demonstrates that the band gap of CDs depends on size. The larger size of CDs (containing larger size of sp2 conjugated domain) results in a narrowing of band gap and a red-shift of PL wavelength. Such a finding is supported by the previous works [15,23]. In addition, another report by Zbořil group has noted that graphitic nitrogen triggers red-shift of PL wavelength in CDs; both experimental characterizations and density functional theory confirm this finding [30]. In our work, the XPS data combined with the Raman result reveal that the amount of graphitic nitrogen increases in the order of v-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs, which leads to the red-shift of PL wavelength. Therefore, according to the detailed characterizations and some important published papers, we propose that the emission wavelength of CDs is mainly regulated by the size of sp2 conjugated domain and the content of graphitic nitrogen. It is worth to mention that the size of sp2 conjugated domain, rather than the size of particle, is really responsible for the quantum confinement effect [41].

As is well known, CDs usually suffer from self-quenching in the solid state owing to direct π–π interactions or excessive resonance energy transfer [45–49], which strictly restricts their wide usage. We embed CDs in starch, a biocompatible solid matrix, to suppress the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching [50,51]. As seen in Fig. 5 a–c, the CD/starch composites exhibit similar PL spectra as compared with the aqueous-state CDs. Under irradiation of a 365-nm UV lamp, bright blue, green, and pink fluorescence are observed by the naked eyes. Remarkably, the quantum yields of v-CD/starch, g-CD/starch, and o-CD/starch are improved to be 20.7%, 12.1%, and 8.9%, respectively. Fig. S5 show the PL decay curves of the CD/starch composites. The average PL lifetimes of v-CD/starch, g-CD/starch, and v-CD/starch are measured to be 4.56, 4.62, and 2.95 ns, respectively, which are essentially the same as those of the aqueous-state CDs. This result suggests that the enhancement in quantum yield from aqueous solution to starch composite is static in nature [52]. CDs have a tendency to collide in solution. However, loading CDs in starch would provide efficient restriction of their movement, resulting in more favorable radiative recombination of electron and hole [53]. The CD/starch composites are outstandingly photostable. The intensities of the solid-state fluorescence remain almost unchanged under 4-hour continuous irradiation with a 450-W mercury lamp. We fabricate multicolor CD/starch-based LEDs by simply coating these phosphors on the commercial UV chips (365 nm) using the AB glue. Fig. 5d–f give the emission spectra and photographs of the as-prepared LEDs, and Fig. 5g–i describe the Commission International de L’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates. It should be noted that after mixing with the AB glue, the surroundings of CDs are changed, and the emissions of CDs in LEDs are red-shifted, which may be ascribed to self-absorption effect and energy transfer of the CD phosphors [54,55]. We find that the g-CD/starch coated LED emits near-white light with CIE of (0.30, 0.33). But its high correlated color temperature (CCT) of 7466 K and low color rendering index (CRI) of 78 hamper the practical application in warm white LED. Excitingly, the LED coated with o-CD/starch exhibits a CIE of (0.41, 0.37), a much lower CCT of 3265 K, and an improved higher CRI of 90, satisfying the standard for indoor illumination (CCT < 4000 K and CRI greater than 80) [4,51].

In summary, a facile method has been developed to prepare colorful CDs by adjusting the pH of 2,3-diaminopyridine solution. The polymerization, crosslinking and carbonization processes of the precursor controlled by pH determine the size of sp2 domain and the content of graphitic nitrogen, resulting in CDs emitting different PL colors from violet to orange. The as-synthesized CDs can be easily dispersed in starch, achieving enhanced quantum yields, strong solid-state fluorescence, and high photostability. Multicolor LEDs based on CD/starch phosphors are successfully fabricated, which offer great potential in practical lighting applications. This work not only provides a new route to fluorescent CDs with tunable PL, but also elucidates the emission mechanism and promotes the utilization of CDs in optical devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2021J075), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.134172.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon Dots: A new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.J. Liu, R. Li, B. Yang, Carbon Dots: A new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications, ACS Cent. Sci. 6 (2020) 2179–2195.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The rapid and large-scale production of carbon quantum dots and their integration with polymers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Y. Du, C.F. Wang, G. Wu, S. Chen, The rapid and large-scale production of carbon quantum dots and their integration with polymers, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 60 (2021) 8585–8595.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as nano-organocatalysts for synthetic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Rosso, G. Filippini, M. Prato, Carbon dots as nano-organocatalysts for synthetic applications, ACS Catal. 10 (2020) 8090–8105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and fabrication of carbon dots for energy conversion and storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Hu, M.Y. Li, J.S. Qiu, Y.P. Sun, Design and fabrication of carbon dots for energy conversion and storage, Chem. Soc. Rev. 48 (2019) 2315–2337.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modulating triplet excited-state energy in phosphorescent carbon dots for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhang, T.T. Li, Y. Zheng, M. Zhang, M.L. Liu, Z.J. Liu, K. Zhang, H.W. Lin, Modulating triplet excited-state energy in phosphorescent carbon dots for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 13 (2021) 43241–43246.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and synthesis of nanosensor based on unsaturated double bond functional carbon dots for phenylephrine detection using bromine as a bridge

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhang, X.C. Li, T.T. Li, M.L. Liu, K. Zhang, Y. Zheng, M.H. Lan, J. Zhang, Z.P. Zhang, Design and synthesis of nanosensor based on unsaturated double bond functional carbon dots for phenylephrine detection using bromine as a bridge, Anal. Chem. 93 (2021) 5145–5150.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-wavelength, multicolor, and white-light emitting carbon-based dots: achievements made, challenges remaining, and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shamsipur, A. Barati, S. Karami, Long-wavelength, multicolor, and white-light emitting carbon-based dots: achievements made, challenges remaining, and applications, Carbon 124 (2017) 429–472.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: materials, synthesis, properties and approaches to long-wavelength and multicolor emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.L. Wu, Z.X. Liu, Y.H. Yuan, Carbon dots: materials, synthesis, properties and approaches to long-wavelength and multicolor emission, J. Mater. Chem. B 5 (2017) 3794–3809.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red carbon dots: optical property regulations and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.J. Zhu, Y.L. Zhai, Z.H. Li, P.Y. Zhu, S. Mao, C.Z. Zhu, D. Du, L.A. Belfiore, J.G. Tang, Y.H. Lin, Red carbon dots: optical property regulations and applications, Mater. Today 30 (2019) 52–79.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Far-red to near-infrared carbon dots: preparation and applications in biotechnology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.X. Shi, H.M. Meng, Y.Q. Sun, L.B. Qu, Y.H. Lin, Z.H. Li, D. Du, Far-red to near-infrared carbon dots: preparation and applications in biotechnology, Small 15 (2019) ,1901507.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with red/near-infrared emissions and their intrinsic merits for biomedical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, X.X. Zhou, J.S. Wei, X.B. Li, B.T. Qin, X.B. Chen, H.M. Xiong, Carbon dots with red/near-infrared emissions and their intrinsic merits for biomedical applications, Carbon 167 (2020) 322–344.
